ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chicken karaage with cheese mayo; closest defensible match is 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03301521). Cheese mayo topping is not captured in the ontology term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible match. The cheese mayonnaise topping and Japanese karaage-style seasoning are not separately encoded in FoodEx2 at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept found for chicken karaage with cheese mayo; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chicken karaage with cheese mayo is approximately 150 g, representing a standard canteen or food-court portion of several pieces of fried chicken topped with cheese mayonnaise sauce.

nutrient_source.title: 702D Chicken Karaage With Cheese Mayo — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record for '702D Chicken Karaage With Cheese Mayo (D)'. All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients are not individually reported in the HPB record and are set to -1 where unavailable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken karaage with cheese mayo. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous food 'fried chicken with batter/coating' from the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The dish has a low carbohydrate content (8.85 g/100g) dominated by potato starch coating and sugar in the marinade; the high fat and protein content from chicken, cheese, and mayonnaise substantially blunts the glycaemic response. GL per 150 g serving = (45 × 8.85 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.97, rounded to 5.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1279 mg per 100 g — more than double the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 1919 mg sodium, representing 83% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2300 mg, and regular consumption poses a serious risk of elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular complications.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (8.85 g/100g) with moderate glycaemic impact, offset by high saturated fat from cheese and mayonnaise which worsens insulin resistance over time
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and high protein (16.09 g/100g) support short-term blood glucose stability, but the high total fat (23.06 g/100g) and estimated saturated fat from processed cheese and mayonnaise can impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size, avoid pairing with high-GI staples such as white rice, and choose this dish only occasionally.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.06 g/100g), high sodium (1279 mg/100g), and estimated significant saturated fat from processed cheese and mayonnaise — all primary cardiovascular risk drivers
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in both sodium and total fat, with saturated fat contributed by processed cheese and mayonnaise; individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia should treat this as an occasional food only, request reduced cheese and mayo portions, and monitor cumulative sodium and saturated fat intake across the day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); high saturated fat from cheese and mayonnaise exceeds RA dietary thresholds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation and high saturated fat content from processed cheese and mayonnaise are primary disqualifiers for rheumatoid arthritis management; individuals with RA should request baked or air-fried karaage with reduced or eliminated cheese mayo topping, and note that sodium at 1279 mg/100g may worsen RA-related joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Karaage with Cheese Mayo is a Japanese-style deep-fried chicken dish served with a rich cheese mayonnaise topping, popular in Singapore food courts and canteens. Karaage refers to the Japanese technique of marinating bite-sized chicken pieces — typically thigh meat — in soy sauce, ginger, garlic, and sake, then coating them in potato starch or flour before deep-frying to a crispy golden exterior with a juicy, tender interior. The cheese mayo variant adds a creamy, tangy topping of processed cheese blended with Japanese-style mayonnaise, which contributes significant fat, sodium, and calcium to the dish. Per 100 g, the dish provides 307 kcal, 16.09 g protein, 23.06 g fat, and 8.85 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 1279 mg driven by soy sauce, processed cheese, and mayonnaise. The calcium reading of 1375 mg/100g is exceptionally high and reflects the processed cheese component. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and fat load, and moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to its high fat content impairing insulin sensitivity despite low carbohydrates. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), with a glycaemic load of approximately 5.9 per 150 g serving. This dish is not suitable for low-sodium, low-fat, or anti-inflammatory diets. It is best consumed occasionally and in controlled portions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore food-court dishes. The record is marked as canonical and reviewed with 0.95 confidence in the source system. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific dish.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record for this dish as of 2026-04-16. Detailed fatty acid fractions and most micronutrient values are not available in the HPB source for this composite dish. 2. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; the starch coating and marinade ingredients contribute negligible fibre. 3. The calcium value of 1375 mg/100g is unusually high and reflects the processed cheese component as reported by HPB; this value is preserved as-is from the canonical source. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on fried chicken with batter from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct GI measurement exists for chicken karaage with cheese mayo. GL per 150 g serving calculated as (45 × 8.85 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.97, rounded to 5.9. 5. The dish name in the input contains a trailing parenthesis '(D' which appears to be a menu code truncation; the canonical food name has been normalised to 'Chicken Karaage with Cheese Mayo'. 6. This dish is classified as Japanese cuisine served in Singapore food courts; multilingual requirement is Japanese native-script name only per G6 rules.
